title: Your first Go program 🐹
instruction:
The front door: `func main()`. Every Go program has one — it's where Go walks in and starts running.

This program already works. Click **Run & check** to see it in action.
successMessage: Boom — you just ran a Go program. That's it. No setup, no config files, no drama.

title: Announce Your Library
instruction: Now make it yours. Change the message from `"Hello, Library!"` to `"Welcome to my library!"`, then add a second `fmt.Println()` below it that prints the hours: `"Open daily from 9 AM to 8 PM"`.
hint: Change the first line to fmt.Println("Welcome to my library!") and add: fmt.Println("Open daily from 9 AM to 8 PM")
successMessage: Your library has a name and hours. The computer reads your code top-to-bottom, like a recipe.

title: Print the Book Count
instruction: The library has 1200 books. Use `:=` to create a variable named `books` with the value `1200`, then print a sentence like `"We have 1200 books!"` using that variable.
successMessage: A variable holds a value so you can reuse it. The box has a label ('books') and a number inside.

title: Meet the Librarian
instruction: Create two variables: `name := "Alice"` (the librarian) and `books := 5000` (the total collection). Print them on separate lines — first her name, then the book count.
successMessage: Two variables, two lines of output. Strings with quotes, numbers without. Go knows the difference.

title: Add a Comment
instruction: Lines that start with `//` are comments — I skip them completely. They're notes for humans. Add a `//` comment above the `fmt.Println()` call. Write something like `// This line prints the time`. Then change the message to print `"Tomorrow at 9 AM"`.
hint: // This line prints the time — or any // comment before the Println call.

codeChecks.message: Add a // comment on its own line above the fmt.Println call.

successMessage: Comments are free text — Go ignores them, humans read them. Use them to explain why, not what.
